Apple___Apple_scab.description: A common fungal disease affecting apple leaves and fruit, causing olive-green to dark scabby lesions that reduce fruit quality and tree vigor.
symptoms: Olive-green to brown velvety spots on leaves
Dark, scabby, corky lesions on fruit surface
Premature yellowing and dropping of leaves
Cracked or deformed fruit in severe cases
Apple___Apple_scab.cause: Fungus Venturia inaequalis, which overwinters in fallen leaves and spreads via wind-blown spores during wet spring weather.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as captan or myclobutanil at green tip stage
Remove and destroy infected leaves and fruit
Prune trees to improve air circulation and reduce humidity
prevention: Plant scab-resistant apple varieties
Rake and dispose of fallen leaves each autumn
Apply preventive fungicide sprays before wet spring periods
Avoid overhead irrigation that prolongs leaf wetness

Apple___Black_rot.description: A fungal disease that causes leaf spots, fruit rot, and cankers on branches, capable of significantly reducing apple yield and fruit storage life.
symptoms: Purple-bordered brown spots on leaves (frog-eye leaf spot)
Concentric brown to black rings on rotting fruit
Sunken, reddish-brown cankers on limbs and trunk
Mummified fruit remaining on the tree
Apple___Black_rot.cause: Fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa, which infects through wounds, dead wood, and natural openings, especially during warm, humid weather.
treatment: Prune out and destroy cankered wood and mummified fruit
Apply fungicides labeled for black rot during the growing season
Remove infected fruit promptly to reduce spore spread
prevention: Maintain tree vigor through proper fertilization and watering
Avoid mechanical injury to bark and limbs
Sanitize orchard by removing dead wood and fallen debris
Ensure good air circulation through proper pruning

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.description: A fungal disease requiring both apple and cedar/juniper hosts to complete its life cycle, producing bright orange lesions on apple leaves and fruit.
symptoms: Bright yellow-orange spots on upper leaf surfaces
Small black dots (spermogonia) within the spots
Orange, tube-like structures on the underside of leaves
Lesions on fruit that can cause distortion
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.cause: Fungus Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae, which alternates between apple trees and juniper/cedar trees to complete its life cycle.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as myclobutanil starting at pink bud stage
Remove nearby juniper or cedar trees when feasible
Prune out visible galls on cedar hosts before spring
prevention: Plant rust-resistant apple varieties
Maintain distance between apple trees and cedar/juniper hosts
Monitor and remove galls on nearby junipers in late winter
Apply preventive fungicide sprays during wet spring conditions

Apple___healthy.description: Apple leaves and fruit showing no signs of disease, pest damage, or nutrient deficiency, indicating good tree health.
symptoms: Uniform green leaf color
No spots, lesions, or discoloration
Normal leaf shape and size
Healthy fruit development
Apple___healthy.cause: Not applicable; represents a disease-free, healthy plant state.
treatment: No treatment necessary
Continue routine monitoring for early signs of disease
prevention: Maintain balanced fertilization and proper watering
Conduct regular inspections for early pest or disease detection
Practice good orchard sanitation
Prune annually to maintain airflow and sunlight penetration

Healthy fruit set
Blueberry___healthy.cause: Not applicable; represents a disease-free, healthy plant state.
treatment: No treatment necessary
Continue routine monitoring for pests and disease
prevention: Maintain acidic, well-drained soil
Water consistently, especially during fruit development
Apply mulch to conserve moisture and suppress weeds
Monitor for early signs of common blueberry diseases

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.description: A fungal disease that produces a white powdery coating on cherry leaves and shoots, affecting photosynthesis and fruit quality.
symptoms: White powdery fungal growth on leaves and shoots
Curling or distortion of young leaves
Stunted shoot growth
Reduced fruit quality and delayed ripening
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.cause: Fungus Podosphaera clandestina, which thrives in warm, dry conditions with high humidity and spreads via airborne spores.
treatment: Apply sulfur-based or other labeled fungicides at first sign of disease
Prune infected shoots to reduce fungal spread
Improve air circulation by thinning dense canopy growth
prevention: Plant resistant cherry varieties where available
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization that promotes lush growth
Space trees adequately for airflow
Apply preventive fungicide treatments during susceptible growth stages

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.description: Cherry tree leaves showing normal color and structure with no visible signs of disease or pest damage.
symptoms: Uniform green leaf coloration
No powdery coating, spots, or curling
Normal shoot growth
Healthy fruit development
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.cause: Not applicable; represents a disease-free, healthy plant state.
treatment: No treatment necessary
Continue routine monitoring for early disease symptoms
prevention: Maintain proper pruning for airflow
Avoid overhead watering to reduce leaf wetness
Monitor regularly during warm, humid weather
Ensure balanced fertilization practices

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.description: A fungal leaf disease that produces rectangular gray to tan lesions on corn leaves, reducing photosynthetic area and yield potential.
symptoms: Small, rectangular tan to gray lesions bound by leaf veins
Lesions that expand and merge, killing large leaf areas
Gray fungal growth visible on lesion surface in humid conditions
Premature leaf death in severe infections
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.cause: Fungus Cercospora zeae-maydis, favored by warm, humid weather and prolonged leaf wetness, often surviving in crop residue.
treatment: Apply foliar fungicides when disease pressure is high
Rotate crops to non-host plants to reduce inoculum
Remove or bury infected crop residue after harvest
prevention: Plant resistant or tolerant corn hybrids
Practice crop rotation with non-host crops
Use tillage to reduce residue-borne inoculum
Avoid dense planting that limits airflow

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.description: A fungal disease causing reddish-brown pustules on corn leaves, which can reduce yield if infection occurs early and severely.
symptoms: Small, reddish-brown, elongated pustules on both leaf surfaces
Pustules that rupture and release rust-colored spores
Leaf yellowing surrounding pustules in heavy infections
Premature leaf senescence under severe disease pressure
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.cause: Fungus Puccinia sorghi, which spreads via windborne spores and thrives in cool, moist conditions.
treatment: Apply fungicides if disease appears early and severely
Monitor fields regularly during humid weather
Remove volunteer corn plants that may harbor the fungus
prevention: Plant rust-resistant corn hybrids
Avoid late planting that increases exposure to spore-producing periods
Scout fields regularly for early detection
Maintain balanced soil fertility to support plant resilience

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.description: A fungal disease producing long, cigar-shaped gray-green lesions on corn leaves, capable of causing significant yield loss under favorable conditions.
symptoms: Long, elliptical, cigar-shaped lesions on leaves
Lesions that turn gray-tan as they mature
Lesions merging to cause large areas of dead leaf tissue
Reduced grain fill in severely affected plants
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.cause: Fungus Exserohilum turcicum, which survives in crop residue and spreads via wind and rain splash during moderate temperatures and high humidity.
treatment: Apply foliar fungicides at early disease onset
Rotate crops to reduce fungal inoculum in soil
Till under or remove infected crop residue
prevention: Plant resistant corn hybrids
Practice crop rotation with non-host species
Avoid continuous corn planting in the same field
Scout fields regularly during the growing season

Corn_(maize)___healthy.description: Corn plant exhibiting normal leaf color, structure, and growth with no signs of disease or pest damage.
symptoms: Uniform green leaf coloration
No lesions, spots, or pustules
Normal stalk and leaf development
Healthy ear formation
Corn_(maize)___healthy.cause: Not applicable; represents a disease-free, healthy plant state.

Grape___Black_rot.description: A destructive fungal disease affecting grape leaves, shoots, and fruit, capable of causing complete crop loss if left untreated.
symptoms: Small brown circular spots on leaves with dark borders
Black, shriveled, mummified berries
Reddish-brown lesions on shoots and tendrils
Tiny black fungal fruiting bodies within lesions
Grape___Black_rot.cause: Fungus Guignardia bidwellii, which overwinters in mummified berries and infected canes, spreading via rain splash during warm, wet weather.
treatment: Apply fungicides on a preventive schedule starting early spring
Remove and destroy mummified berries and infected plant material
Prune out infected canes during dormancy
prevention: Practice thorough vineyard sanitation
Ensure good canopy airflow through proper pruning and training
Remove wild or abandoned grapevines nearby
Apply protective fungicide sprays during susceptible growth stages

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).description: A complex fungal trunk disease that causes leaf discoloration, berry spotting, and vine decline, often leading to sudden vine death.
symptoms: Tiger-stripe pattern of yellow and brown discoloration on leaves
Dark spotting on berries resembling measles
Internal wood discoloration and decay
Sudden wilting and vine collapse in severe cases
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).cause: A complex of fungi including Phaeomoniella chlamydospora and Phaeoacremonium species, which infect through pruning wounds and colonize the vine's woody tissue.
treatment: Remove and destroy severely infected vines
Apply wound protectants after pruning
Practice remedial surgery on trunks with limited infection
prevention: Prune during dry weather to reduce infection risk
Disinfect pruning tools between cuts
Avoid large pruning wounds when possible
Use certified disease-free planting material

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.description: A fungal disease causing angular brown spots on grape leaves, which can lead to premature defoliation and reduced vine vigor.
symptoms: Angular reddish-brown spots on leaves
Yellowing tissue surrounding lesions
Premature leaf drop in severe cases
Dark fungal growth on the underside of leaves
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.cause: Fungus Pseudocercospora vitis (formerly Isariopsis leaf spot fungus), favored by warm, humid conditions and prolonged leaf wetness.
treatment: Apply appropriate fungicides during periods of high humidity
Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves
Improve canopy ventilation through pruning
prevention: Maintain good vineyard sanitation
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness duration
Practice proper canopy management for airflow
Monitor vines regularly during wet seasons

Grape___healthy.description: Grapevine displaying normal leaf color and structure with no visible signs of disease or pest damage.
symptoms: Uniform green leaf coloration
No spots, lesions, or discoloration
Normal shoot and tendril growth

Grape___healthy.cause: Not applicable; represents a disease-free, healthy plant state.

Maintain proper canopy management

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).description: A severe bacterial disease spread by psyllid insects that causes yellowing, misshapen fruit, and eventual tree decline, with no known cure.
symptoms: Blotchy yellow mottling on leaves that is asymmetrical across the midrib
Small, lopsided, bitter-tasting fruit
Premature fruit drop
Yellow shoots and twig dieback
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).cause: Bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus, transmitted by the Asian citrus psyllid insect vector.
treatment: Remove and destroy infected trees to limit disease spread
Apply insecticides to control psyllid populations
Support tree health with balanced nutrition to prolong productivity
prevention: Use certified disease-free planting stock
Monitor and control psyllid vectors regularly
Inspect groves frequently for early symptoms
Remove abandoned or unmanaged citrus trees nearby

Peach___Bacterial_spot.description: A bacterial disease that causes lesions on peach leaves, fruit, and twigs, reducing marketable yield and tree vigor.
symptoms: Small, angular, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn purple-brown
Shot-hole appearance as lesion centers fall out
Dark, sunken spots on fruit surface
Cankers on twigs during dormant season
Peach___Bacterial_spot.cause: Bacterium Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni, spread by wind-driven rain and favored by warm, wet conditions.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides during dormant and early growing season
Prune out infected twigs and cankers
Avoid working in orchards when foliage is wet
prevention: Plant resistant peach varieties where available
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Space trees properly for good air circulation
Remove and destroy fallen infected leaves and fruit

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.description: A bacterial disease causing leaf and fruit spotting in bell peppers, which can significantly reduce marketable yield in warm, humid climates.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown
Yellow halos surrounding leaf lesions
Raised, scab-like spots on fruit
Defoliation in severe infections

treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides at early disease onset
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Avoid overhead irrigation to limit bacterial spread
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Rotate crops with non-host plants
Avoid working in fields when plants are wet
Disinfect tools and equipment between uses

Potato___Early_blight.description: A common fungal disease causing target-like leaf spots in potatoes, which can reduce yield if it develops early in the season.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target-board pattern)
Yellowing of tissue surrounding lesions
Premature defoliation starting from lower leaves
Dark, sunken lesions on tubers in severe cases
Potato___Early_blight.cause: Fungus Alternaria solani, favored by warm temperatures, alternating wet and dry periods, and plant stress.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil or azoxystrobin at first symptom appearance
Remove and destroy severely infected foliage
Maintain adequate plant nutrition to reduce stress
prevention: Practice crop rotation with non-host plants
Avoid overhead irrigation to limit leaf wetness
Use certified disease-free seed potatoes
Maintain proper plant spacing for airflow

Potato___Late_blight.description: A highly destructive fungal-like disease responsible for historic famines, capable of destroying entire potato crops within days under favorable conditions.
symptoms: Water-soaked, pale-to-dark green lesions on leaves
White fungal growth on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Rapid blackening and collapse of foliage
Reddish-brown rot on tubers
Potato___Late_blight.cause: Oomycete Phytophthora infestans, which spreads rapidly in cool, wet weather via airborne spores.
treatment: Apply fungicides preventively before disease onset in high-risk conditions
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Avoid irrigation that prolongs leaf wetness during outbreaks
prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed potatoes
Use resistant potato varieties when available
Monitor weather conditions for late blight risk periods
Destroy volunteer potato plants and cull piles

Squash___Powdery_mildew.description: A common fungal disease producing a white powdery coating on squash leaves, reducing photosynthesis and overall plant vigor.
symptoms: White, powdery fungal patches on leaf surfaces
Yellowing and browning of affected leaves
Stunted plant growth in severe infections
Premature leaf death

Remove and destroy severely infected leaves
Improve air circulation by spacing plants properly
prevention: Plant powdery mildew-resistant squash varieties
Avoid overcrowding plants
Water at the base rather than overhead
Monitor plants regularly during warm, humid weather

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.description: A fungal disease causing purplish spots on strawberry leaves that can merge and give a scorched appearance, reducing plant vigor and yield.
symptoms: Small, irregular purple spots on leaves
Spots merging into larger scorched-looking blotches
Leaves appearing dried or burned at edges
Reduced runner and fruit production in severe cases
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.cause: Fungus Diplocarpon earlianum, which overwinters in infected leaves and spreads via rain splash during wet weather.
treatment: Apply labeled fungicides at early disease symptoms
Remove and destroy infected leaves after harvest
Improve air circulation through proper plant spacing
prevention: Use disease-resistant strawberry varieties
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness
Practice good field sanitation and residue removal
Rotate strawberry beds every few years

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: A bacterial disease causing leaf and fruit spotting in tomatoes, which thrives in warm, wet conditions and can severely reduce marketable yield.
symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn dark brown
Yellow halos around leaf lesions
Raised, scabby spots on fruit
Defoliation in severe infections
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.cause: Bacterium Xanthomonas species, spread through contaminated seed, rain splash, and infected transplants.
treatment: Apply copper-based bactericides at early symptom onset
Remove and destroy infected plant debris
Avoid working with plants when foliage is wet
prevention: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Rotate crops with non-host plants
Avoid overhead irrigation
Disinfect tools and stakes between uses

Tomato___Early_blight.description: A widespread fungal disease causing target-like leaf spots in tomatoes, often starting on older leaves and progressing upward.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target pattern)
Yellowing of surrounding leaf tissue
Lower leaves affected first, progressing upward
Dark, sunken lesions on stems and fruit
Tomato___Early_blight.cause: Fungus Alternaria solani, favored by warm temperatures, humidity, and plant stress or nutrient deficiency.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil or copper-based products
Remove and destroy severely infected leaves
Maintain proper plant nutrition to reduce susceptibility
prevention: Practice crop rotation with non-host plants
Mulch around plants to reduce soil splash onto leaves
Stake or cage plants to improve air circulation
Water at the base rather than overhead

Tomato___Late_blight.description: A highly destructive disease capable of rapidly killing tomato plants under cool, wet conditions, and closely related to the pathogen responsible for the Irish potato famine.
symptoms: Large, water-soaked, greenish-black lesions on leaves
White fungal growth on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Rapid blackening and collapse of foliage and stems
Firm, brown, greasy-looking lesions on fruit
Tomato___Late_blight.cause: Oomycete Phytophthora infestans, which spreads quickly via airborne spores in cool, moist weather.
treatment: Apply fungicides preventively when conditions favor disease development
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Avoid overhead watering during outbreaks
prevention: Use certified disease-free transplants
Plant resistant tomato varieties when available
Monitor local disease forecasts and weather conditions
Ensure good air circulation through proper spacing and staking

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: A fungal disease common in greenhouse and humid environments, causing yellow leaf spots and characteristic mold growth on leaf undersides.
symptoms: Pale green to yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces
Olive-green to grayish-brown mold on the underside of leaves
Leaf curling and wilting in severe infections
Premature leaf drop
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.cause: Fungus Passalora fulva (formerly Fulvia fulva), favored by high humidity and poor air circulation, especially in greenhouses.
treatment: Apply fungicides labeled for leaf mold control
Improve ventilation to reduce humidity levels
Remove and destroy infected leaves
prevention: Use resistant tomato varieties where available
Increase plant spacing for better airflow
Avoid overhead watering and reduce greenhouse humidity
Practice good sanitation by removing plant debris

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.description: A common fungal disease producing numerous small spots on tomato leaves, often leading to significant defoliation if untreated.
symptoms: Small, circular spots with dark borders and gray centers
Tiny black fungal structures visible within spots
Yellowing and browning of affected leaves
Severe defoliation starting from lower leaves
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.cause: Fungus Septoria lycopersici, which spreads through rain splash, contaminated tools, and infected plant debris.
treatment: Apply fungicides such as chlorothalonil or copper-based products
Remove and destroy infected lower leaves
Avoid overhead irrigation to limit spore spread
prevention: Practice crop rotation with non-host plants
Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash
Stake plants to improve airflow
Remove plant debris at the end of the season

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.description: Damage caused by tiny sap-sucking mites that create stippling and webbing on tomato leaves, often thriving in hot, dry conditions.
symptoms: Fine yellow or white stippling on leaves
Fine webbing on the underside of leaves and between stems
Leaves turning bronze or gray with heavy infestation
Premature leaf drop under severe infestation
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.cause: Infestation by Tetranychus urticae (two-spotted spider mite), which thrives in hot, dry, dusty conditions and reproduces rapidly.
treatment: Apply miticides or insecticidal soap targeting spider mites
Introduce natural predators such as predatory mites
Rinse plants with water to dislodge mites and reduce populations
prevention: Maintain adequate soil moisture to reduce plant stress
Avoid excessive dust accumulation on plants
Monitor plants regularly during hot, dry weather
Encourage beneficial insect populations in the garden

Tomato___Target_Spot.description: A fungal disease producing target-like lesions on tomato leaves, stems, and fruit, capable of causing significant yield loss in warm, humid climates.
symptoms: Brown lesions with concentric rings resembling a target
Lesions on stems and petioles causing girdling
Sunken, dark lesions on fruit
Severe defoliation in advanced infections
Tomato___Target_Spot.cause: Fungus Corynespora cassiicola, favored by warm temperatures and high humidity, spreading via wind and rain splash.
treatment: Apply fungicides labeled for target spot control
Remove and destroy severely infected plant material
Improve air circulation through proper staking and spacing
prevention: Practice crop rotation with non-host plants
Avoid overhead irrigation to reduce leaf wetness
Remove plant debris after harvest
Monitor fields regularly during warm, humid periods

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.description: A viral disease transmitted by whiteflies that causes severe stunting and leaf curling in tomatoes, often resulting in substantial yield loss.
symptoms: Upward curling and cupping of leaves
Yellowing along leaf margins and between veins
Severe stunting of plant growth
Reduced flowering and fruit set
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.cause: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV), transmitted primarily by the silverleaf whitefly (Bemisia tabaci).
treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants to reduce virus spread
Apply insecticides to control whitefly populations
Use reflective mulches to deter whiteflies
prevention: Plant virus-resistant tomato varieties
Use insect-proof netting or row covers on young plants
Control weeds that may harbor whiteflies
Monitor and manage whitefly populations early in the season

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.description: A viral disease causing mottled leaf discoloration and stunted growth in tomatoes, easily spread through contact and contaminated tools.
symptoms: Mottled light and dark green patterns on leaves
Leaf curling, distortion, and fern-like narrowing
Stunted overall plant growth
Reduced fruit yield and quality
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.cause: Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV), spread mechanically through contaminated hands, tools, and infected seed.
treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants promptly
Disinfect tools and hands after handling infected plants
Avoid tobacco use near plants, as it can carry related viruses
prevention: Use certified virus-free seed and transplants
Practice strict sanitation and tool disinfection
Avoid handling plants when wet
Control weeds that may serve as virus reservoirs
